questions.question: Define a quadratic equation and give two examples.
questions.answer: A quadratic equation is an equation of degree two. Its standard form is ax² + bx + c = 0, where a ≠ 0. Examples include x² − 5x + 6 = 0 and 2x² + 3x − 1 = 0.

questions.question: Explain the standard form of a quadratic equation.
questions.answer: The standard form of a quadratic equation is ax² + bx + c = 0, where a, b and c are real numbers and a ≠ 0.

questions.question: Explain why a quadratic equation is called a second-degree equation.
questions.answer: It is called a second-degree equation because the highest power of the variable is two.

questions.question: Explain the graphical representation of a quadratic equation.
questions.answer: The graphical representation of a quadratic equation is a parabola drawn on the coordinate plane.

questions.question: Explain what is meant by the roots of a quadratic equation.
questions.answer: The roots of a quadratic equation are the values of the variable for which the equation becomes zero.

questions.question: Explain the nature of roots when the discriminant is positive.
questions.answer: When the discriminant is positive, the quadratic equation has two distinct real roots.

questions.question: Explain the nature of roots when the discriminant is zero.
questions.answer: When the discriminant is zero, the quadratic equation has two equal real roots.

questions.question: Explain the nature of roots when the discriminant is negative.
questions.answer: When the discriminant is negative, the quadratic equation has no real roots.

questions.question: Explain the quadratic formula.
questions.answer: The quadratic formula is x = [−b ± √(b² − 4ac)] / 2a. It is used to find the roots of any quadratic equation.

questions.question: Explain factorisation method of solving quadratic equations.
questions.answer: In factorisation method, the quadratic equation is written as a product of two linear factors and each factor is equated to zero.

questions.question: Explain completing the square method.
questions.answer: In completing the square method, the quadratic equation is converted into a perfect square to find the roots.

questions.question: Explain why the graph of y = x² opens upwards.
questions.answer: Because the coefficient of x² is positive, the parabola opens upwards.

questions.question: Explain why the graph of y = −x² opens downwards.
questions.answer: Because the coefficient of x² is negative, the parabola opens downwards.

questions.question: Explain the meaning of the vertex of a parabola.
questions.answer: The vertex of a parabola is the turning point where the graph changes direction.

questions.question: Explain the axis of symmetry of a parabola.
questions.answer: The axis of symmetry is a vertical line that divides the parabola into two identical halves.

questions.question: Explain why a quadratic equation has at most two roots.
questions.answer: Because the degree of a quadratic equation is two, it can have at most two roots.

questions.question: Explain the relationship between roots and x-intercepts of a parabola.
questions.answer: The roots of a quadratic equation are the x-coordinates where the parabola intersects the x-axis.

questions.question: Explain why quadratic equations are important in mathematics.
questions.answer: Quadratic equations are important because they are used in algebra, geometry, physics and real-life problem solving.

questions.question: Explain one real-life application of quadratic equations.
questions.answer: Quadratic equations are used to calculate maximum height in projectile motion problems.

questions.question: Explain why quadratic formula is called a universal method.
questions.answer: Because it can be used to solve any quadratic equation.

questions.question: Explain the term discriminant.
questions.answer: The discriminant is the expression b² − 4ac used to determine the nature of roots.

questions.question: Explain why completing the square method is useful.
questions.answer: It helps convert the quadratic equation into a solvable perfect square form.

questions.question: Explain the role of coefficient a in a quadratic equation.
questions.answer: The coefficient a determines the direction and width of the parabola.

questions.question: Explain why quadratic equations are polynomial equations.
questions.answer: Because they involve variables raised to non-negative integer powers only.

questions.question: Explain the educational importance of studying quadratic equations.
questions.answer: They prepare students for higher mathematics and real-life applications.
